title: Number Systems: Binary and Hexadecimal
summary: Positional number systems, binary and hex conversion, bitwise operations.
content_html:
Number Systems: Binary and Hexadecimal

All information inside a computer is stored and processed as numbers. To fully understand how computers work, you must become comfortable with several different number systems — particularly binary (base 2) and hexadecimal (base 16). These are not just abstract mathematical concepts; they are the actual languages that hardware speaks.

Positional Number Systems

The decimal system you use every day is a positional number system. Each digit’s value depends on both its face value and its position within the number. The position determines a power of the base by which the digit is multiplied.

For example, in decimal (base 10): 347 means 3 × 102 + 4 × 101 + 7 × 100 = 300 + 40 + 7 = 347.

This principle generalises to any base. For base b, the value of a number with digits dn...d1d0 is: dn × bn + ... + d1 × b1 + d0 × b0.

Binary (Base 2)

Binary uses only the digits 0 and 1. Each binary digit is called a bit. To convert a binary number to decimal, multiply each bit by the corresponding power of 2 and sum the results.

Example: 010011102

  • 0 × 27 = 0 × 128 = 0
  • 1 × 26 = 1 × 64 = 64
  • 0 × 25 = 0 × 32 = 0
  • 0 × 24 = 0 × 16 = 0
  • 1 × 23 = 1 × 8 = 8
  • 1 × 22 = 1 × 4 = 4
  • 1 × 21 = 1 × 2 = 2
  • 0 × 20 = 0 × 1 = 0

Sum: 64 + 8 + 4 + 2 = 78 in decimal.

To convert decimal to binary, repeatedly divide by 2 and record remainders from bottom to top. For example, 78 ÷ 2 = 39 r0, 39 ÷ 2 = 19 r1, 19 ÷ 2 = 9 r1, 9 ÷ 2 = 4 r1, 4 ÷ 2 = 2 r0, 2 ÷ 2 = 1 r0, 1 ÷ 2 = 0 r1. Reading remainders upward: 1001110 (add leading zero: 01001110).

Hexadecimal (Base 16)

Hexadecimal uses 16 symbols: 0-9 and A-F, where A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15. Its primary purpose in computing is as a compact representation of binary data.

The critical insight: 24 = 16, so exactly 4 bits map to one hex digit. This makes conversion between hex and binary trivial:

  • Split binary into groups of 4 from the right.
  • Replace each group with its hex equivalent.

Example: 0100 11102 = 4E16 because 0100 = 4 and 1110 = 14 = E.

Hex notation conventions: 0x4E (C/programming style), 4EH (assembly style), or #4E (some assemblers). In this course you will see all three forms.

Converting Between Number Systems

A helpful summary of common conversions:

  1. Binary to Decimal: Sum (bit × power of 2) for each position.
  2. Decimal to Binary: Repeated division by 2, remainders give bits from LSB to MSB.
  3. Binary to Hex: Group 4 bits, convert each group.
  4. Hex to Binary: Expand each hex digit to 4 bits.
  5. Hex to Decimal: Sum (digit × power of 16) for each position.

Bitwise Operations

Computers can perform bitwise operations that treat a number as a sequence of individual bits. These operations are fundamental to low-level programming and hardware design:

  • NOT (unary): Inverts every bit. NOT 1010 = 0101. Also written as overbar or tilde (~).
  • AND (binary): Output is 1 only when both inputs are 1. 1010 AND 1100 = 1000.
  • OR (binary): Output is 1 when at least one input is 1. 1010 OR 1100 = 1110.
  • NAND: AND followed by NOT. Output is 0 only when both inputs are 1.
  • NOR: OR followed by NOT. Output is 1 only when both inputs are 0.
  • XOR (Exclusive OR): Output is 1 when inputs differ. 1010 XOR 1100 = 0110.

These operations have direct hardware implementations as logic gates, and in software they are used for tasks like masking bits (AND), setting bits (OR), toggling bits (XOR), and checking flags.

Why Not Just Use Decimal?

You might wonder why computers don’t simply use the familiar decimal system. The reason is purely physical: it is straightforward to build circuits with two reliable states (high voltage/low voltage), but very difficult to distinguish ten different voltage levels reliably. Digital (binary) systems are far more noise-resistant and easier to manufacture at scale. Hexadecimal is then used purely for human convenience when reading or writing binary data.

Practical Examples

Understanding these number systems is essential for everyday computing tasks such as reading memory addresses (e.g., 0x7FFF0000), interpreting color codes in web design (e.g., #FF5733), reading network IP addresses in hex, and understanding error codes returned by operating systems. Programmers who work close to the hardware must be fluent in binary and hexadecimal.

exercises.title: Binary to Decimal
exercises.prompt: What is the decimal value of the binary number 10110100?

exercises.title: Hex to Binary
exercises.prompt: What is the binary representation of the hexadecimal value 0xB3?

exercises.title: Bitwise AND
exercises.prompt: What is the result of performing a bitwise AND on 1111 0000 and 1010 1010?

exercises.title: Hex Digits per Byte
exercises.prompt: How many bits does a single hexadecimal digit represent?
